title: 2025/12/04 - JU (Part 3)
displayTitle: 2025-12-04 - Justice Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Executive Board for the Elimination of Family Violence and Sexual Violence [Part 3]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Executive Board for the Elimination of Family Violence and Sexual Violence
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Justice Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Executive Board for the Elimination of Family Violence and Sexual Violence Submitters: Centre for Family Violence and Sexual Violence Prevention Emma Powell, Chief Executive Andrew Kibblewhite, Chief Executive Tusha Penny, Acting Deputy Commissioner, Central and Southern Districts, NZ Police Darrin Haimona, Deputy Chief Executive, Enabling Communities, Oranga Tamariki Mark Henderson, General Manager Safe, Strong Families and Communities, Ministry of Social Development Aphra Green, Deputy Chief Executive, Policy, Data and Insights, Social Investment Agency Petition of David White MNZM: Action to Prevent Family and Sexual Violence in New Zealand Submitters: David White Centre for Family Violence and Sexual Violence Prevention
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Executive Board for the Elimination of Family Violence and Sexual Violence. Centre for Family Violence and Sexual Violence Prevention. Emma Powell, Chief Executive

chunks.text: All of the money that goes into this is obviously spent in other agencies rather than in, uh, the Center. Uh, everyone in this room is acutely aware of the horrors and harm caused by family violence and sexual violence in our country. It includes high-profile tragedies as well as the tens of thousands of New Zealanders whose names we don't always learn, who experience violence on a regular basis, usually behind closed doors. When someone makes the difficult decision to leave a violent relationship or get help in the aftermath of sexual assault, they need a safe and

appropriate response from trained professionals who understand the complexities of family violence and sexual violence, and who can work with them to provide support and make a safety plan. Family and sexual violence are some of the most entrenched problems we face. They can't be solved by any one government or government agency, but we can make traction if we work together.

Uh, Te Orirekura is a 25-year strategy for intergenerational change, and the executive board is committed to building the necessary foundations to realize that shared vision. Uh, during 24-25, the executive board agreed core operating procedures to try and sharpen up each agency's responsibilities, including expectations about information sharing.

Uh, the executive board, um, uh, set, presented new strategic intentions to the House, and they set out where we will focus our efforts in the medium term, including system stewardship, evidence-based investment, and better outcomes for people affected by violence. So, I will, uh, a- almost finish my opening remarks and hand over to Emma.

I would just, in closing, say family violence and sexual violence are one of the main drivers of violent crime in New Zealand. The data shows that a significant percentage of victims have experienced multiple victimizations. Family violence is a pattern of behavior, and the cycle is difficult to break entirely. By working together and sustaining our focus, the executive board can use a variety of levers across a range of key agencies to improve crisis response, build prevention system (coughs) , excuse me, systems that keep people safer. Emma has delegated

day-to-day operational responsibility for the Center. Uh, uh, Emma, some of you will have clocked, uh, has just announced that she'll finish up, uh, with the Center in March, uh, next year. So, uh, this will probably be her last time in this capacity to p- present in front of, uh, this committee. I would just acknowledge the h- the huge amount of effort, uh, and achievement that Emma has put in and, um, and had over the last four years, more or less, in this role.

And then I'll pass over to Emma, who will also make a few quick remarks, uh, and then we can get into the questions, uh, proper. Thank you, Andrew, and, uh, kia ora koutou. Um, it is really great to be with you today because it's so important that we get to discuss, um, well these issues. Uh, the Center is the operational face and lead of the coordination, um, and at times wrangling of all of the agencies, uh, to get on the same page about what's most important, what's gonna make a difference, and how we align our action and

activity to get there. Um, the Center has been working quite hard in this last year to deliver our new action plan, uh, which was launched in December last year. We've become a lot more focused into three core areas, uh, of work. One of those is to really understand, uh, where we are investing, uh, and what that looks like across the whole portfolio. So we've been able to, for the first time, visualize all of the money that's going into family violence and sexual violence in, in a direct capacity, and start to understand

where the gaps are, what that looks like across the country, and what we need to do as a result of that. And I'm sure there'll be a great opportunity to discuss some of that. The other big focus has been on, uh, what we call keeping people safe, which is what is actually happening on the ground in community. Uh, we, we seek to work in a multi-agency way here in Wellington, but we have to ensure, uh, in community, where things are actually closest to whānau, that those agencies are coming together with the NGOs and specialists to make a difference.

And so we've been doing a huge amount of work this year to strengthen those responses. And so again, I know in our agenda, we have a chance to, to go through a lot of that. The other area of focus for us has been around stopping violence. Um, I would say that not enough focus and attention has been placed to date on the effectiveness, uh, and getting it right for the users of violence or perpetrators of harm, and that's, uh, that's the third key area, um, for us, um, this year. Um, one of the things that we, uh, at the

Center are working, uh, to try and achieve as well is to really look to set the standards, the capabilities and expectations of our workforces. Um, and so we've been able to deliver this year, um, a new risk, uh, safety practice framework. Um, and next year, um, early in the new year, we'll be delivering our sexual violence capability frameworks.

And so again, a chance to talk to that. Um, the last thing I just wanted to note, um, as well, uh, was just like a, a really special mention to, uh, the providers, the workforce, and importantly to acknowledge victim-survivors, uh, their voices, and the importance of keeping those central to the work that we do.

Uh, we have continued to partner this year with the Backbone Collective to, to continue to seek voices, experiences of victim-survivors. And if I say nothing else today, um, in my time here in, in my role is to listen to the voices of those that most matter, um, which is those victim-survivors and their families, and we will absolutely have the solutions to these challenges.

So thank you very much. Um, and I just acknowledge also my team, um, and the hard work that they put into this area. Good. Thank you. Right. Now we're gonna turn to the audit, uh, report. And Mr. Steventon. Oh, gosh, the audit report. Yep. Right.... got the letter again. Well, I mean, yeah, you've got, you've got three goods from the Office of the, uh, Auditor General.

I mean is there anything you wanna kind of call out where you think there are areas for improvement from the audit report from your perspective? Well, um, I think- Mm-hmm.... it's been a really... I always find these processes really useful for us. Um, we're ever needing to explain and understand how we do things better in, in the way that we set up and operate. Um, I think that couple of the things that we've managed to do this year is really strengthen our risk framework, um, and our risk reporting, uh, not only for the center but how that

applies to the delivery of the action plan. Our success rests on the success of other agencies, uh, being aligned and making sure that we are, um, have the right capacity and capability in place to do that. So, that's certainly been something that we've, um, im- improved upon, I think, this year. Um, Andrew and I are often talking about how to continuously evolve and mature and, um, improve the function, um, and, uh, performance of the IEB. You'll note in our questions and answers that we provided you, that we are s- very interested in looking at our

performance measures, and we might get a chance to talk about that as well. It's been something that has been set around delivery of the action, stewarding the strategy, and ministerial satisfaction. Uh, we have our outcome and measurement framework, but getting to the core of the performance of the IEB is probably something for us to continue to focus on.

Just one quick follow-up. Mm-hmm. Uh, just, uh, could just... I'll just so we're all just on, all on the same page- Yeah. Obviously, we've got, we've got the annual report here. We've got the board as of the 30th of June in here. Any changes to the board makeup- Have we got-... from that? No, not that. Not, mm... No. No. So, the board hasn't changed. The board is set in the- Okay.

... Public Service Act. Yeah. What the board has chosen to do this year is to create a sub-committee. So, that's one of my- Yeah. That was going to be my next question- Yeah. ... 'cause obviously, we don't have the Police Commissioner here or, um, you know, the Director-General of Health, et cetera. So, maybe just briefly for the committee, yeah, how is it then operationalized? 'Cause obviously, you've got, you've got the board and obviously got the chair here, which is great. But yeah, how then is it operationalized 'cause obviously,

they've got- Yeah. ... other colleagues. Yeah. Yeah. So, I'll talk, I'll talk at a board level. Mm-hmm. We, we have, um, the, the board as chief executives. Yeah. It does include the Police Commissioner and the like. The board is meeting, I think, three or four times a year as a full board, probably three times a year. Yep. Uh, w- we've, we have on a more or less a monthly basis met as a sub-committee of the board.

Mm-hmm. Uh, which, uh, has been, uh, me and Emma. It has been the Police Commissioner, the, um, chief executive of Social Development, Debbie Power, the head of Oranga Tamariki, uh, which has been Andrew, uh, Bridgeman and- Corrections.... uh, Jeremy Lightfoot, uh, from Corrections and the chief executive of the Social Investment Agency. So, that, that has been the subgroup. Uh, that's been a subgroup because there's been a particular focus on, uh, the action plan with they're the main players and particularly on multi, uh, agency responses, which we'll talk about a little bit

more. So, we've, we've kind of recognized that there's a... this i- this is a big and complex thing and- Yeah.... health has got a lot and education. They're all still members of the board. They, they come together, uh, less frequently, but to really just do their checking in, in, in the s- some of the more f- formal worki- of a board and do a little bit of scanning.

So, in the new year, we will be having a discussion as a board, uh, I think it was our full board, which will be looking at the sexual, uh, violence, because, you know, that's an area where I think that, uh, that hasn't been one of the, the top priorities in the current action plan that is clearly an, an, an issue that, that is, that is, you know- (coughs)... (coughs) is need of a lot of, uh, work.

So, we will do that at a, at a full board. Uh, at a subgroup, we are keeping track of, of, uh, the, the things that we have to be delivering- Yeah.... more now. And then- Mm-hmm. ... there's a dep sec, uh, group which Emma might talk to. A number of our colleagues behind us- Yeah.... uh, would be present as part of that dep secs group which, which is works in really close support- Yeah.

... of the board and of the center. Mm-hmm. That, that's right. So, we convene the deputy secretaries, um, uh, monthly. And some of that's about making sure what's coming up to the board, everyone is across and comfortable with and has their hand on. Um, but some of that too is to take away key pieces of work- Sure.... and make it, make it happen.

Yeah. Um, it is a really good clearinghouse, but also a place to, I think, continue to push our ambition and our level of change and the things that we are wanting to push the board on. So, when we do that collectively, uh, I think we, we get there with more of a unified voice. Good, okay. Okay, I'm going to Karl, yes, Bates, and then I'm gonna come to Miss White.

Thanks, Jim. Just talking about the performance reporting, how far along that journey of getting them clear, um, are you? Yeah. So, during the year, we did a piece of work, uh, in, in conjunction with the deputy chief executives to start to look at, under all of the functions of the board, how would we translate those into two things? One was, uh, what does, uh, better maturity look like against each of those functions?

That's things like our- Mm-hmm.... stewardship of the strategy, our alignment on policy, our outcomes, our stakeholder engagement and trust with the sector 'cause that's really key. So, we looked at a range of those areas, um, and then from there, we also created some indicators. So, we've been doing that work as agencies, um, and then the next step is to take a refined version of that to the board so that they're comfortable with that.

And so, when do you think that'll become publicly available? Um, so we're working on that over the next couple of months. I think there's a window in the early part of next year, uh, to make those, to put those forward. Oh, okay, okay. I think we would then pilot those measures to make sure that they're the- Do you have a board level or are you go- ... right kinds of measures before bringing them into effect sort of next financial year.

I th- There's a couple other kind of measurement frameworks as well. One is obviously the government's target, the target fall which goes to the, uh, victims of arma- Yep. ... fening. Within that, we track the-... uh, family violence and sexual violence, uh, victims. So we're, we're tracking it there. And then there's the monitoring and outcomes framework, which is a publication that we put the first version of that out in, uh, about this time last year. Uh, it's due to come out every two years.

We're actually gonna do the next one in March to get on a, a March cycle rather than a December cycle. Okay. And so that will give you an overview which will really present the challenge. Are we making impact at the outcomes level- Yeah.... on family violence and sexual violence? So that'll be the overarching one, and what Emma's describing is being able to be just sharper on the performance as it relates to the specific things that are sitting underneath that.

What you're actually doing, isn't it? Yeah. Right, we're meant to be migrating towards strategic direction, but I've got Ms. White. Um, I wanna know about, um, the work with the Social Investment Agency. Um, new player and, um, you talked about it at the time of the budget, um, e- at the center was working on the Social Investment Agency, with the Social Investment Agency to align the plan and make investments work.

Can you tell me a little bit about that? But before you do, Emma, it's lovely to see you. Mm-hmm. And I am really sorry that you're leaving. Yeah. Thanks. Thanks for all the work. Yeah. I'm, I might invite Afra Green to join us at the table, um, uh, for this. Uh, I'm, I, I might start, lead, lead off with, so Afra deeps, uh, Sec at the Social Investment Agency.

I might, might kick off, uh, uh, in responding to that just to recognize the kind of the, at a board level, the, the points of connection. The Social Investment Agency is the vehicle we're, uh, we're a new... Basically the, the kind of flagship of the, a new approach thinking about the government social policy is being carried forward.

It's thinking about how we contract, it's understanding what is investment that is going in. Uh, it, it really is the place where we would be able to remove duplication and get much more effective coordination of services, and understand what impact they would have. So that is a, that's, that's kind of a, a, a bit of DNA, uh, that has to be intertwined with what we do in the- Agreed.

... in, in the, um, family and sexual violence, uh, space. So that is kind of where it begins. We've done work, and Emma might want to talk about this a little bit more, in, uh, that we haven't published, uh, as yet. But it looks at, uh, all of the expenditure in the family and sexual violence space. And you are into hundreds of millions of dollars obviously, and trying to understand that better.

That's, it's work that we've done very much collectively with the Social Investment Agency as a platform from which we will actually be able to, I think, bring a much richer analysis. And, and deal with some of the problems that I know, just from talking to Mr. White outside, are really prevalent. That there is duplication, that it's often hard to get things done, that often agencies on the ground are gonna be tripping over themselves.

There's a lot of the work that Afra might want to talk to the Social Investment Agency is thinking about in that space as well. But Emma, do you wanna have a first crack at it? Yeah, and I think it's been really important that we're very transparent about what that partnership looks like, 'cause I know some of our sector and providers are sort of saying, "Well, how does this all work?" And, um, essentially for the board at the beginning of the year, we're really cautious, uh, to not race into, uh, adopting too quickly a social investment approach perhaps where it

didn't, um, where it wasn't going to be effective, or where we needed to really retain particular ways of operating, or services and supports that were really fundamental to safety first. And so we did, we have taken a few months to really understand that picture, uh, which is really where the baseline review insights information has come from, uh, to get that really good picture. Um, where the partnership works well is that Social Investment Agency are developing the tools and the approaches around outcome-based contracting, uh, making much sharper

the data leads, um, and insights, um, sort of visible and, and measurement and, and progress, um, monitoring against interventions. And so we sort of, our role is to s- is to steer where, where best practice is, where, uh, I guess also really to call out the watch-outs and to, you know, be cautious about certain things.

And then to work together to say, "Well, how can we use the toolset to advance better outcomes, particularly for key groups, um, uh, within, uh, you know, family violence and sexual violence?" Um, I know there's been a lot of focus on children and young people so far- Mm-hmm.... um, in terms of those new investments, and that's really critical to our, our outcomes as well. Yeah. Um, uh, I think, um, A- Andrew and Emma have had, covered quite a lot.

Um, the, the core areas for the Social Investment Agency obviously are in creating the tools and frameworks, uh, for other agencies to use. And, um, that's what we've done particularly in looking across the investment, uh, in, in relation to family and sexual violence. And then, uh, with the Social Investment Fund, um, the cohorts that we, um, have, the Social Investment Fund ministers have selected, we take a deliberate view of what is the long term- Line.

... investment, um, picture for those cohorts and, and how are they impacted, um, in a range of ways. Yeah. Sorry. Now that, we'll move on. Uh, did you want Social Investment? Actually yeah, I'm happy to leave the audit stuff and just, I've got two supplementaries on this if that's okay.

Oh, on Social Investment? Yeah. Yeah. And then I'll come back. We'll take that and then we're gonna come back. Yeah. Yeah, good. Okay. I'm sure we'll be along- Just for the new members, I want to complete lines of questioning, then we come back to a new topic. So Social Investment. Fantastic. Thank you all for being here. Uh, the annual report notes that the center has been focused on social investment, and we know there's been that work done with, um, in partnership with the Social Investment Agency.

And priority one of the second action plan is investing and commissioning well. And the phase one review of past spending is now complete, so what lessons were learned in this review? And can we expect to see any new or different commissioning or budgeting in the future as a result of this?

Mm-hmm. Yeah, so that first phase review is done between, um, both our agencies, and it was one of the first sort of cabs off the rank to start to say, "How might we apply some of the social investment outcome sort of, uh, toolset-" Mm-hmm. "... to our area?" So I just wanna note, it was a desktop exercise. It wasn't going out and deeply evaluating each of those services.

Right. Um, but what that helped us to see, um, is always more work to be done, uh, to understand effectiveness. Uh, but we can start to see that there are certainly programs and services with a clear theory of change, um, a clear line of sight for who those services are for, and the kinds of outcomes that are being intended.

Mm-hmm. Um, things like, uh, child advocates and women's refuge- Mm-hmm.... were demonstrated to be, you know, well, well-positioned, ensuring that we had stopping violence services for boys and men, um, and, uh, a range of other sort of areas that needed to be supported.

Multi-agency responses, as an example, was, were shown to be effective, um, in parts, and we'll no doubt will talk a bit about that. Um, and then there was some other areas that were identified that, um, it wasn't because they were failing, it was just there was not enough evidence at this stage- Mm-hmm.... to tell us, um, that. And so, I think what we've learned, um, one of the big things is, is that you can't just look at the dollars. You have to look at effectiveness- Mm-hmm.

... at the same time. We are probably less strong, um, in, uh, available data on effectiveness than we would like to be, and we need to continue to, to build that. Um, we've kicked off an evaluation, for example- (coughs) ... into multi-agency responses- Mm-hmm.... on the ground to start to bring those voices through to understand how effective they are. So, we, we have taken some of that and, and brought it through.

Thank you. Uh, so can the sector be sure that no reductions in service provisions will occur as part of the reviews? And what provisions has the Center made to ensure that the providers are being kept informed? Mm-hmm. Yeah, yeah. As you said, trust is so important about, um, the intentions and approach so that we don't have a similar situation to the Oranga Tamariki contracting disaster.

Yeah. Um, so two, two questions there. One is that, um, our role is to make visible, uh, where the spending is, to give guidance and advice to the board about where best to, um, prioritize and give effect to best value for their funding. Mm-hmm.

Um, the individual contracting decisions still remain with the individual agencies- (coughs)... uh, and so I think that's probably a continuing point of, uh, discussion and tension at times. Um, then secondly- Just, just before we move on to the second one, one of the things I would note is we, we were chased around a bit by this committee last year, because, uh, of decisions. There was debate on some of the decisions police had taken, debate on some of the decisions Oranga Tamariki was, was taking. And the, the question was, "What are you doing about it?" In the

Center- Yeah. And, and so this is a little bit of this tension that, um, Emma is alluding to. I don't have, neither does Emma, decision rights over what all of the individual agencies are doing. We have agreed and quite formally agreed as a board a set of protocols about making sure that, uh, each CE and each agency, uh, has an obligation to inform the Center so that we can be much better placed, uh, to understand what those impacts are.

But y- we won't be able to give you a, an undertaking from here- Mm-hmm.... that there won't be any changes to other agencies' contractings that might have an impact on, on services. Our job will be to make sure we are better sited on those when they come in and understand the implications of it. (inaudible) Yeah. Okay. Thank you. All right. We're coming back - Sorry?

Did you want me to answer the second part? Okay. (laughs) Thank you. Yes. That's what we're doing, social investment. Um, so, uh, one of the primary ways... So we, we have a number of networks and communication channels that we do communicate through. One thing that we've done with our... We have a systems working group. There are about 50 to 60 people that we convene monthly to bring together, um, from all facets of the sector.

Um, we've run some joint sessions between SIA and the Center about exactly that, lifting the veil. What's the, um, the partnership, how we're working, and what can they expect to see? Yeah. So appreciate early on, it was probably a little bit misty, um, for people and now we are, um, as I say, working much more...

A- as I think each of us has got more clear about those roles we are communicating there. Okay. Dylan? All right. Um, I just wanna ask about, um, the new, um... Like, you had the little subcommittees, and they met, and they talked about how there was gonna be a commitment to sharing information. And, um, that seemed to me an interesting...

I'm, I'm taking your points about your role advocating to ministers, but sharing information seems quite passive in some ways. It's not saying, "Tell us when you think there's harm going to be done." Yeah, so the protocols that the... that we've set out for the board, uh, have a range of things.

And one of those is not just to inform but to consult and demonstrate implications and impact. So, um, if, if a decision is being taken, particularly around contracting resourcing, it might be in terms of function of an agency's role when it comes to family violence or sexual violence, that actually that's needing to come with, uh, an impact sort of, um, assessment done against that as well.

I love the idea of multi-agency, things like this, but let's take a couple of real examples. One is the police withdrawal from, um, family harm incidents.... and that, can you tell me how that would work with this? And another would be the issue of, of a ban on homelessness. Two examples that are very current that may well impact on the levels of violence that are taking place.

I might answer it in a general way, and then I'll get Tasha to talk specifically to the, uh, police one. Uh, in a general way, the, the, the board sub-committee is meeting right alongside the social sector chief executives, and so there is a clearing house of opportunity to talk about, uh, are there, are there new things presenting, new issues arising that, that we should collectively be, uh, concerned about. If the, if there is a, a, a decision taken on homelessness, that you wouldn't necessarily expect that to immediately

come, uh, before the board. You, you, you'd want to... Uh, we would, to be honest, would want to see what a, what the specific implications of it would be, that the center might get in, uh, consulted on some of the cabinet papers that were going up and would be able to put a perspective in there, as would other agencies. But it would be, it would be through that sort of standard, uh, process. But I wonder, Tasha, do you want to talk about the police?

Because that's, that was an issue last year, I'm sure it's an issue now, but yeah, in terms of- Mm. ... police engagement. I'd be in real trouble if I didn't ask you. Mm-hmm. Yeah, yeah, no, kia ora, everyone. Um, uh, and thanks for the opportunity. I think, um, language is really important, so withdrawal isn't the right language. Mm-hmm. Um, so when we, um, adopted the RISS Calm Attendance Framework, we actually did a pilot, as you probably know, in September '24, and then it was rolled out in December '24.

So like a lot of change, you need to be really confident and clear on why you are doing it. And, you know, the reality is, we have to ensure... Family harm is a significant issue across Aotearoa, and has been for a long, long time, which is why we've, uh, done things like ISR. And we've really challenged ourselves in the multi-agency space, and to your point, of absolutely sharing information.

So the reality is, in the, in the, with high demand and volume, you just have to make sure that every agency is doing an appropriate first risk assessment, and then appropriate service to what's required. So if we are talking about the RH, HA, I really do, uh... You know, it is, it is not a withdrawal, and my evidence of that is that we have an additional 7.7% of family harm which we have said are P1. So P1 is the highest response and prioritization then you can, that you can get from a triple one call. It actually means that it i- there is no bigger

priority. You have to have available calls and you must attend. So again, since we've made this change, it's seven point cent- seven percent, um, increase. What we assure everyone, though, is that we don't just leave, uh, calls for service. We, uh, prioritize them, we triage them, we push them back into the districts- Mm.

... and then we look at, what is the appropriate thing to do? Mm. And a large percentage of those will actually go to multi-agency tables, where they can have a very big look at, uh, the whole family and everyone's information that they know. Okay. Uh, just clarification. Mm-hmm.

Since this re-prioritization, I think you said that your responses to Priority One family violence incidents has gone up seven percent. Does that suggest to you that not attending lower level incidents results in more Priority One very dangerous incidents?

No, no, it doesn't mean that at all. Wh- why not? What's the ca- what's the cause and effect there? Yeah. And Dr. Webb, I love that you are interested in this, um, because of course in your area, we have ISR, and I know you've been and had a look at that, at how effective it is. So no, I wasn't saying that at all. What I was saying is, when we're doing a P1 and we're getting their, the information, uh, we will, we will make sure that we attend wherever there's a breach around the criminal justice proceeding, around

PSO or any warrants. We're looking for harm. If we, there is any indication at all that there is harm to any persons or property, we will absolutely attend. So why is there a seven percent increase in those kind of most serious incident? Well, uh, we'd have to say, and we have done an initial evaluation, we'd have to say that, uh, because we've also enhanced the training of the call takers, and we've really challenged ourselves on making sure that we're asking the right questions at that time and seeking the right information, that actually we are in a far better position from the

risk assessment framework to really ascertain quite quickly, both looking at the past history but looking at what's being presented at the moment, to actually decipher if there is immediate risk or a immediate breach of any criminal justice proceedings and we must attend. So we are viewing it as actually good cholesterol to make sure that those who need us in that moment, that we are doing a P1.

So you're saying that you, you're attending more... There are more P1 incidents because you're better at detecting Priority One incidents? We would say that that is definitely a factor, because I mean, it's no different if we look at the agency and what one of the key priority is around training the frontline services and workforces. This would be a natural, uh, thing that we would expect.

The more we- Uh, but so you can assure us- Yeah. Oh sorry, if I can just finish? No, that's okay. The more we train and the more we ensure that people are understanding the nature of family harm, which again has been a significant e- uh, factor for a long time, this is what we would hope to see. So... Last one. The, uh... Yeah, thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you for that indulgence.

So notwithstanding the fact that there are seven percent more Priority One incidents, you're saying that this is because you pick them up better and not because there are more dangerous incidents occurring?That seems to be counterintuitive. No, it, it could be possibly an and-and. I'm not gonna discount that. But what I will say is whenever we have seen a focus on training and specialization in a risk matrix, we are better at then applying a graduated response model and ensuring appropriate service, opposed to every single

call we get for family harm, we have to attend when we've got a significant demand for service. Okay. So, we've done the police call-out to family harm events.
